# An ultraflexible energy harvesting-storage system for wearable applications

[*Nature Communications*](/ncomms)
**volumeВ 15**, ArticleВ number:В 6546 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

14k Accesses

48 Citations

28 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The swift progress in wearable technology has accentuated the need for flexible power systems. Such systems are anticipated to exhibit high efficiency, robust durability, consistent power output, and the potential for effortless integration. Integrating ultraflexible energy harvesters and energy storage devices to form an autonomous, efficient, and mechanically compliant power system remains a significant challenge. In this work, we report a 90вҖүВөm-thick energy harvesting and storage system (FEHSS) consisting of high-performance organic photovoltaics and zinc-ion batteries within an ultraflexible configuration. With a power conversion efficiency surpassing 16%, power output exceeding 10вҖүmWвҖүcmвҖ“2, and an energy density beyond 5.82вҖүmWh cmвҖ“2, the FEHSS can be tailored to meet the power demands of wearable sensors and gadgets. Without cumbersome and rigid components, FEHSS shows immense potential as a versatile power source to advance wearable electronics and contribute toward a sustainable future.

## Introduction

Flexible electronics have produced a paradigm shift in the wearable technology sector[1](#ref-CR1 "Gao, W., Ota, H., Kiriya, D., Takei, K. & Javey, A. Flexible electronics toward wearable sensing. Acc. Chem. Res. 52, 523вҖ“533 (2019)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Someya, T., Bao, Z. & Malliaras, G. G. The rise of plastic bioelectronics. Nature 540, 379вҖ“385 (2016)."),[3](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR3 "Rogers, J., Bao, Z. & Lee, T. W. Wearable bioelectronics: opportunities for chemistry. Acc. Chem. Res. 52, 521вҖ“522 (2019)."). Remarkable advancements were made in developing wearable sensors that are thin, conformal, and stretchable[4](#ref-CR4 "Perez, A. J. & Zeadally, S. Recent advances in wearable sensing technologies. Sensors 21, 6828 (2021)."),[5](#ref-CR5 "Luo, Y. et al. Technology roadmap for flexible sensors. ACS Nano 17, 5211вҖ“5295 (2023)."),[6](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR6 "Ates, H. C. et al. End-to-end design of wearable sensors. Nat. Rev. Mater 7, 887вҖ“907 (2022)."). In particular, ultraflexible devices have garnered immense attention due to their ability to seamlessly integrate with the human skin[7](#ref-CR7 "Jiang, Z. et al. Ultraflexible integrated organic electronics for ultrasensitive photodetection. Adv. Mater. Technol 6, 2000956 (2021)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Wei, B. B. et al. Ultraflexible tattoo electrodes for epidermal and in vivo electrophysiological recording. Cell Rep. Phys. Sci. 4, 23 (2023)."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Miyamoto, A. et al. Inflammation-free, gas-permeable, lightweight, stretchable on-skin electronics with nanomeshes. Nature Nanotech. 12, 907вҖ“913 (2017)."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Someya, T. et al. A large-area, flexible pressure sensor matrix with organic field-effect transistors for artificial skin applications. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 101, 9966вҖ“9970 (2004)."),[11](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR11 "Wang, W. et al. Neuromorphic sensorimotor loop embodied by monolithically integrated, low-voltage, soft e-skin. Science 380, 735вҖ“742 (2023)."), providing more accurate data acquisition and an enhanced degree of user comfort.

Advances in wearable technology are highly dependent on the development of flexible energy devices, which should offer high efficiency, durability, and constant power output and possess the capacity for effortless integration[12](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR12 "Liu, R., Wang, Z. L., Fukuda, K. & Someya, T. Flexible self-charging power sources. Nat. Rev. Mater. 7, 870вҖ“886 (2022)."),[13](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR13 "Lu Yin, J. W. Wearable energy systems: what are the limits and limitations? Natl. Sci. Rev. 10, nwac060 (2023)."). Most commercial wearables rely on bulky coin cells or rechargeable batteries as power sources. These contribute significantly to the systemвҖҷs overall rigidity, posing limitations to their mechanical compliance and often necessitating frequent charging or replacement.

The next frontier is to produce ultraflexible energy sources, especially flexible energy harvesting-storage systems (FEHSSs) that efficiently generate and store power, and adapt to curved surfaces and moving objects. While there has been remarkable progress in ultraflexible energy harvesters, integrating these with flexible energy storage devices to form an efficient and robust power system brings a set of challenges. These include achieving outstanding power conversion and storage efficiency without compromising the mechanical compliance, establishing a stable interfacial connection, and realizing human-friendly user interface.

There is growing interest and progress in integrated FEHSSs. Self-powered energy modules like thermoelectric generators, bioenergy harvesters that involve triboelectric and piezoelectric generators, and photovoltaic-supercapacitor combinations, have demonstrated early success in this field[13](#ref-CR13 "Lu Yin, J. W. Wearable energy systems: what are the limits and limitations? Natl. Sci. Rev. 10, nwac060 (2023)."),[14](#ref-CR14 "Yu, H., Li, N. & Zhao, N. How far are we from achieving selfвҖҗpowered flexible health monitoring systems: an energy perspective. Adv. Energy Mater. 11, 2002646 (2021)."),[15](#ref-CR15 "Liu, R. et al. An efficient ultra-flexible photo-charging system integrating organic photovoltaics and supercapacitors. Adv. Energy Mater. 10, 2000523 (2020)."),[16](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR16 "Liu, R. et al. Silicon nanowire/polymer hybrid solar cell-supercapacitor: a self-charging power unit with a total efficiency of 10.5%. Nano Lett 17, 4240вҖ“4247 (2017)."). Documented devices typically exhibit a thickness in the range of 1вҖүmm to 600вҖүВөm, with a power output below 5вҖүmWh cmвҖ“2 [17](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR17 "Lee, Y.-H. et al. Wearable textile battery rechargeable by solar energy. Nano Lett 13, 5753вҖ“5761 (2013)."),[18](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR18 "Um, H.-D. et al. Monolithically integrated, photo-rechargeable portable power sources based on miniaturized Si solar cells and printed solid-state lithium-ion batteries. Energy Environ. Sci. 10, 931вҖ“940 (2017)."). The current level of flexibility and efficiency within these systems is very limited, necessitating extensive enhancements to ensure their effective interplay with ultraflexible sensors, and the soft, dynamic attributes of the human body.

Organic photovoltaics (OPVs) featuring lightweight, biosafety, and typically short energyВ payback time are especially suitable for wearable applications[19](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR19 "Tsang, M. P., Sonnemann, G. W. & Bassani, D. M. Life-cycle assessment of cradle-to-grave opportunities and environmental impacts of organic photovoltaic solar panels compared to conventional technologies. Sol. Energy Mater. Sol. Cells 156, 37вҖ“48 (2016)."). Recent investigations have substantially escalated the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of flexible OPVs, propelling it beyond the 10% threshold[20](#ref-CR20 "Qin, F. et al. Robust metal ion-chelated polymer interfacial layer for ultraflexible non-fullerene organic solar cells. Nat. Commun. 11, 4508 (2020)."),[21](#ref-CR21 "Sun, L., Fukuda, K. & Someya, T. Recent progress in solution-processed flexible organic photovoltaics. npj Flex. Electron. 6, 89 (2022)."),[22](#ref-CR22 "Xu, X. et al. Thermally stable, highly efficient, ultraflexible organic photovoltaics. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 115, 4589вҖ“4594 (2018)."),[23](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR23 "Lee, H. et al. Ultra-flexible semitransparent organic photovoltaics. npj Flex. Electron. 7, 27 (2023)."). Simultaneously, intense efforts have been made to enhance the performance robustness to withstand harsh outdoor conditions[22](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR22 "Xu, X. et al. Thermally stable, highly efficient, ultraflexible organic photovoltaics. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 115, 4589вҖ“4594 (2018)."),[24](#ref-CR24 "Jinno, H. et al. Stretchable and waterproof elastomer-coated organic photovoltaics for washable electronic textile applications. Nat. Energy 2, 780вҖ“785 (2017)."),[25](#ref-CR25 "Nam, M. et al. Long-term efficient organic photovoltaics based on quaternary bulk heterojunctions. Nat. Commun. 8, 14068 (2017)."),[26](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR26 "Xiong, S. et al. Waterproof and ultraflexible organic photovoltaics with improved interface adhesion. Nat. Commun. 15, 681 (2024)."). However, ultraflexible OPVs that exhibit the best performance are mostly individual units with a limited size. Ultraflexible OPVs and modules with an active area >вҖү5вҖүcm2 and an areal power output >вҖү10вҖүmWвҖүcmвҖ“2 are still quite rare. Besides, energy derived solely from OPVs has intermittent availability under fluctuating light conditions, which undermines their efficacy in integrated wearable systems that require constant power sources.

Lithium (Li)-ion or Li-polymer pouch cells serve as prevalently used energy storage devices. However, they have to be encapsulated in a strong foil to meet safety standards. Their irregular deformation during wear can lead to cracks and delamination of the battery electrodes, resulting in diminished electrochemical properties and posing a potential risk of toxic liquid electrolyte leakage[27](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR27 "Cha, H., Kim, J., Lee, Y., Cho, J. & Park, M. Issues and challenges facing flexible lithium-ion batteries for practical application. Small 14, 1702989 (2018)."). For electronics to be worn by people, whether on-skin or in-textiles, the emphasis on safety is even greater. As a result, safer battery systems, e.g., zinc (Zn)-ion batteries[28](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR28 "Xiao, X. et al. An ultrathin rechargeable solid-state zinc ion fiber battery for electronic textiles. Sci. Adv. 7, eabl3742 (2021)."),[29](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR29 "Weng, G., Yang, X., Wang, Z., Xu, Y. & Liu, R. Hydrogel electrolyte enabled highвҖҗperformance flexible aqueous zinc ion energy storage systems toward wearable electronics. Small 19, e2303949 (2023).") (ZIB), would facilitate a user-friendly design in a thin configuration. To achieve ultra-flexibility in an integrated FEHSS, the thickness of each individual component needs to be tailored to balance the electrochemical performance, mechanical properties, and durability[30](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR30 "Kong, L., Tang, C., Peng, H. J., Huang, J. Q. & Zhang, Q. Advanced energy materials for flexible batteries in energy storage: A review. Smart Mat. 1 (2020)."). The use of gel and solid-state electrolytes in batteries has indubitably contributed to their flexibility. These electrolytes must simultaneously serve as the separator while preserving adequate ionic conductivity, an intricate task. In other words, developing ultrathin electrolytes poses an ongoing challenge due to the dual requirement of sustaining conductivity and managing the heightened risk of potential short-circuiting[31](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR31 "Xu, K. Nonaqueous liquid electrolytes for lithium-based rechargeable batteries. Chem. Rev. 104, 4303вҖ“4418 (2004)."). Therefore, there persists a demand for flexible energy storage solutions, particularly focusing on the development of thin, efficient, and safe electrolytes and electrodes.

The primary challenge in system integration involves reconciling the disparate components while accounting for their diverse physical, optoelectronic, and electrochemical properties. This process also seeks to generate high voltage output across both the energy harvesting and storage modules. A 650 Вөm-thick FEHSS was demonstrated, consisting of OPVs and a textile-based Li-ion battery[17](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR17 "Lee, Y.-H. et al. Wearable textile battery rechargeable by solar energy. Nano Lett 13, 5753вҖ“5761 (2013)."). The use of a textile battery indeed improved the conformability of the overall system compared to a foil battery. In a different approach, homo-tandem OPVs were used to double the open circuit voltage output to 1.92вҖүV, facilitating the charging of Li-ion and Na-ion batteries[32](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR32 "Hoefler, S. F. et al. New solar cellвҖ“battery hybrid energy system: integrating organic photovoltaics with Li-ion and Na-ion technologies. ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 8, 19155вҖ“19168 (2020)."). A monolithically integrated photo-rechargeable power source was developed using Si photovoltaics and Li-ion batteries[18](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR18 "Um, H.-D. et al. Monolithically integrated, photo-rechargeable portable power sources based on miniaturized Si solar cells and printed solid-state lithium-ion batteries. Energy Environ. Sci. 10, 931вҖ“940 (2017)."). A bipolar stacked solid-state battery configuration was used, resulting in an overall voltage output of 5.4вҖүV from the battery module. Despite the scarce efforts devoted toward merging the energy harvesting and storage components, their potential to enable compliant, efficient, and stable wearable technology remains largely unexplored.

In this work, we present a 90 Вөm-thick, highly efficient, fully integrated energy harvesting and storage system that meets the needs discussed above. It is made possible by ultrathin, high-performance OPV modules paired with thin Zn-ion batteries. First, the ultraflexible OPV cells have a PCE of up to 16.18%, a shelf lifetime exceeding 1500вҖүh, and retain over 92 % of their initial PCE under a continuous illumination of 50вҖүmWвҖүcmвҖ“2 for 500вҖүh. The freestanding 4 Вөm-thick OPV modules have state-of-the-art areal power outputs exceeding 10вҖүmWвҖүcmвҖ“2. Second, a notable innovation in the battery is that we have realized a drastic reduction of the hydrogel electrolyte thickness, from millimeters to 10вҖүВөm, while maintaining its high electrochemical performance. Third, we designed ultraflexible organic diodes with a rectification ratio in the order of 105 in the Вұ 2вҖүV range, to effectively prevent undesired current reflux from the battery to the OPVs in dark conditions. The integrated system has an energy density greater than 5.82вҖүmWh cmвҲ’2, and an overall conversion and storage efficiency of 6.91%, along with excellent operational and storage stability. Finally, we demonstrate an all-in-one energy harvesting and storage system to power wearable electronics, including wearable biosensors, small gadgets like smartwatch and smartphone, etc. Our FEHSS represents a potentially universal power source that can be effortlessly integrated on both human body and textiles, paving the way for a more connected, seamless experience with wearable technology.

## Results

### Design of the FEHSS

FigureВ [1A, B](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1) show an illustration of the structure and the material composition of our FEHSS. The ultraflexible OPV module (chemical structure of the active materials in Fig.В [1C](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1)), with a total thickness of 4вҖүВөm, is positioned atop the flexible Zin-ion battery (composition detailed in Fig.В [1D](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1)). The two components are connected in parallel by an anisotropic conductive film (ACF) tape. Ultrathin (1.5вҖүВөm) parylene films serve as the passivation layer for the OPV and the battery, effectively creating segregation between their active materials. The resulting FEHSS has a thickness of approximately 90вҖүВөm, one of the thinnest structures in documented energy systems with exceptional mechanical compliance. It can be feasibly attached to the human skin (Fig.В [1E](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1)) or textiles (Fig.В [1F](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1)). Bent to a radius of less than 1вҖүmm for 500 cycles or being compressed to a strain of 10% for 100 cycles, the FEHSS maintains over 80% of its overall power conversion and storage efficiency (Fig.В [1G](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1)), showing mechanical durability for practical applications.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-50894-w/MediaObjects/41467_2024_50894_Fig1_HTML.png)

**A** Illustration of the FEHSS functioning as a power source for an on-skin electrocardiogram (ECG) sensor patch for healthcare monitoring. **B** Schematic of the layered configuration of the flexible OPV module and Zn-ion batteries. **C** The chemical structures of the primary donor (PM6) and acceptor (Y6) materials, and the third component (O-IDTBR), forming the active layer of the flexible OPV. **D** Schematic of the Zn/MnO2-graphite battery, with the components of the hydrogel electrolyte highlighted. **E** Photograph showing a 90 Вөm-thick FEHSS attached to the skin of a volunteerвҖҷs shoulder. Scale bar: 2вҖүcm. **F** Photograph showing the same FEHSS attached to a textile. Scale bar: 1вҖүcm. **G** Mechanical durability of the FEHSS for over 500 cycles under a 1вҖүmm bending radius (left), and 100 cycles of compression-stretching under 10% strain (right). The y-axis shows the normalized efficiency of photo-conversion and storage. Inset schematics show the process of bending and compression test cycles. Source data are provided as a Source Data file.

### High-performance ultraflexible OPVs

To construct highly efficient OPVs, we use poly[(2,6-(4,8-bis(5-(2-ethylhexyl-3-fluoro) thiophen-2-yl)-benzo[1,2-b:4,5-bвҖҷ] dithiophene))-alt-(5,5-(1вҖҷ,3вҖҷ-di-2-thienyl-5вҖҷ,7вҖҷ-bis(2-ethylhexyl) benzo[1вҖҷ,2вҖҷ-c:4вҖҷ,5вҖҷ-cвҖҷ] dithiophene-4,8-dione)] (PBDB-T-2F or PM6), a well-known derivative from the poly[(2,6-(4,8-bis(5-(2-ethylhexyl)thiophen-2-yl)-benzo[1,2-b:4,5-bвҖҷ]dithiophene))-alt-(5,5-(1вҖҷ,3вҖҷ-di-2-thienyl-5вҖҷ,7вҖҷ-bis(2-ethylhexyl)benzo[1вҖҷ,2вҖҷ-c:4вҖҷ,5вҖҷ-cвҖҷ]dithiophene-4,8-dione)] (PBDB-T) family, as the donor material[33](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR33 "Zhang, M., Guo, X., Ma, W., Ade, H. & Hou, J. A large-bandgap conjugated polymer for versatile photovoltaic applications with high performance. Adv. Mater 27, 4655вҖ“4660 (2015)."), and 2,2вҖІ-((2Z,2вҖІZ)-((12,13-bis(2-ethylhexyl)-3,9-diundecyl-12,13-dihydro-[1,2,5]thiadiazolo[3,4-e]thieno[2,вҖң3вҖІвҖҷ:4вҖҷ,5вҖІ]thieno[2вҖІ,3вҖІ:4,5]pyrrolo[3,2-g]thieno[2вҖІ,3вҖІ:4,5]thieno[3,2-b]indole-2,10-diyl)bis(methanylylidene))bis(5,6-difluoro-3-oxo-2,3-dihydro-1H-indene-2,1-diylidene))dimalononitrile (BTP-4F or Y6) as the acceptor material (Fig.В [1C](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1))[34](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR34 "Yuan, J. et al. Single-junction organic solar cell with over 15% efficiency using fused-ring acceptor with electron-deficient core. Joule 3, 1140вҖ“1151 (2019)."). They provide an outstanding performance, most notably, a PCE surpassing 19% in a rigid OPV configuration[35](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR35 "Ding, G. et al. Solid additive-assisted layer-by-layer processing for 19% efficiency binary organic solar cells. Nano-Micro Lett. 15 (2023)."). While the system shows promise, it has a relatively limited stability[36](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR36 "Mateker, W. R. & McGehee, M. D. Progress in understanding degradation mechanisms and improving stability in organic photovoltaics. Adv.Mater. 29, 1603940 (2017)."). With increasing time and under continuous illumination, there is a marked loss of PCE, which can be ascribed to aging-induced morphological changes and the formation of light-induced traps that increase the probability of recombination[36](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR36 "Mateker, W. R. & McGehee, M. D. Progress in understanding degradation mechanisms and improving stability in organic photovoltaics. Adv.Mater. 29, 1603940 (2017).").

To improve the inherent stability, we added (5Z)-3-ethyl-5-[[4-[15-[7-[(Z)-(3-ethyl-4-oxo-2-sulfanylidene-1,3-thiazolidin-5-ylidene)methyl]-2,1,3-benzothiadiazol-4-yl]-9,9,18,18-tetra(nonyl)-5,14-dithiapentacyclo[10.6.0.03,10.04,8.013,17]octadeca-1(12),2,4(8),6,10,13(17),15-heptaen-6-yl]-2,1,3-benzothiadiazol-7-yl]methylidene]-2-sulfanylidene-1,3-thiazolidin-4-one (O-IDTBR, Fig.В [1C](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig1)), into the above mix[37](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR37 "Gasparini, N. et al. Exploiting ternary blends for improved photostability in high-efficiency organic solar cells. ACS Energy Lett. 5, 1371вҖ“1379 (2020)."). This effectively broadens the light absorption range (FigureВ [S1A](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#MOESM1)) and leads to an energy cascade path in the bulk heterojunction (Fig.В [2A](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2)) that facilitates exciton dissociation[38](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR38 "Zhu, Y. et al. Rational strategy to stabilize an unstable highвҖҗefficiency binary nonfullerene organic solar cells with a third component. Adv. Energy Mater. 9, 1900376 (2019)."). Moreover, the inclusion of additional active components in a bulk heterojunction is known to aid in reducing the number of trap sites, thus decreasing the recombination probability and increasing charge collection efficiency[39](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR39 "Gasparini, N., Salleo, A., McCulloch, I. & Baran, D. The role of the third component in ternary organic solar cells. Nat. Rev. Mater. 4, 229вҖ“242 (2019).").

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-50894-w/MediaObjects/41467_2024_50894_Fig2_HTML.png)

**A** Energy level alignment of PM6, Y6, and the additive O-IDTBR in the active layer. **B** *J-V* characteristics of ultraflexible OPVs based on a PM6:Y6 binary blend (black) and a PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary blend (red). **C** EQE spectrum of ultraflexible OPVs based on a PM6:Y6 binary blend (black) and a PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary blend (red). **D** Light intensity dependence of *V*OC for OPVs based on a PM6:Y6 binary blend (black) and a PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary blend (red). **E** Light intensity dependence of *J*SC for OPVs based on a PM6:Y6 binary blend (black) and a PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary blend (red). **F** 2D GIWAXS data of PM6:Y6 binary blend. **G** 2D GIWAXS data of PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary blend. **H** Cake cuts from 2D GIWAXS data of the binary blend (black) and the ternary blend (red). Cake cuts in in-plane and out-of-plane directions are shown in dashed and solid lines, respectively. **I** Stability evaluation of PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary OPVs under different conditions, including in inert atmosphere in the dark (green), in ambient conditions in the dark (blue), and under a continuous illumination of 50вҖүmWвҖүcmвҖ“2 in ambient conditions (red). **J** *J-V* characteristics of OPVs with pure ZnO (red) and EDT-ZnO (blue) as the electron transport layer. **K** Light intensity dependence of *V*OC for PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary OPVs with pure ZnO (red) and EDT-ZnO (blue) as the ETL. **L** Photo-stability evaluation of ultraflexible PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 OPVs with ZnO-EDT as the ETLВ (blue), comparedВ to the counterpart deviceВ usingВ ZnO as the ETL (red). The devices were placed under continuous light illumination at 50вҖүmWвҖүcmвҖ“2 for over 500вҖүh. Source data are provided as a Source Data file.

Representative current density-voltage (*J-V*) characteristics for the ultraflexible OPVs based on PM6:Y6 binary and PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary blends are shown in Fig.В [2B](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2). The statistical analysis of their performance is summarized in FigureВ [S1B](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#MOESM1) and TableВ [S1](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#MOESM1). Ternary-blend OPVs show notable performance improvement in current density (*J*SC) and fill factor. For the best-performing ultraflexible devices, *J*SC reaches 25.2вҖүmAвҖүcmвҖ“2, and the fill factor reaches 73.8%, surpassing the values of 23.5вҖүmAвҖүcmвҖ“2 and 73% observed from the binary-blend counterpart devices. The open-circuit voltage (*V*OC) has a moderate increase of 0.02вҖүV, reaching 0.82вҖүV in ternary-blend devices. As a result, the ternary-blend OPV has a superior PCE of 15.2%, surpassing the 13.7% of its binary-blend counterparts вҖ“ a comparison made while both systems are in an ultraflexible configuration (TableВ [S1](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#MOESM1)). From the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra (Fig.В [2C](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2)), the ternary-blend OPV exhibits a high EQE (75вҖ“83%) in the wavelength range of 550вҖүnm to 850вҖүnm, while the binary-blend counterpart shows a lower EQE (70вҖ“78%) in the wavelength range from 400вҖүnm to 850вҖүnm.

The performance of OPV devices is highly dependent on the exciton dissociation and charge recombination processes[40](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR40 "Bartesaghi, D. et al. Competition between recombination and extraction of free charges determines the fill factor of organic solar cells. Nat. Commun. 6, 7083 (2015)."). To probe the charge recombination in the OPVs, we evaluated the light intensity dependence of *J*SC and *V*OC. The ideality factor (*n*) of photovoltaics, which indicates the measure of how closely they follow the ideal diode equation[41](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR41 "Caprioglio, P. et al. On the origin of the ideality factor in perovskite solar cells. Adv. Energy Mater. 10, 2000502 (2020)."), can be derived from the dependence of *V*OC on incident light intensity following:

where *k*B is for the BoltzmannвҖҷs constant, *T* is temperature, *q* is elementary charge, and *I* stands forВ light intensity. The ideality factor was estimated as 1.30 for PM6:Y6 binary-blend OPVs and 1.22 for PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary-blend OPVs, as shown in Fig.В [2D](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2). The ideality factor, observed to be closer to 2 in this binary case, suggests that the dominant carrier loss mechanism is trap-assisted recombination. *J*SC depends on the illumination intensity following the power law,

where *J*SC is the short circuit current density and *Оұ* indicates the extent of bimolecular recombination loss in the devices[42](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR42 "Koster, L. J. A., Kemerink, M., Wienk, M. M., MaturovГЎ, K. & Janssen, R. A. J. Quantifying bimolecular recombination losses in organic bulk heterojunction solar cells. Adv.Mater. 23, 1670вҖ“1674 (2011)."). For the PM6:Y6 binary-blend and PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary-blend OPVs, we found respective *Оұ* values of 0.95 and 0.99, as shown in Fig.В [2E](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2). The observed increase in *Оұ* following the incorporation of O-IDTBR, confirms an increased charge collection efficiency and a reduced incidence of bimolecular recombination. The results highlight the presence of trap sites in both devices and that the binary OPVs have a larger loss of carriers through trap-assisted charge recombination[43](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#ref-CR43 "Zeiske, S. et al. Direct observation of trap-assisted recombination in organic photovoltaic devices. Nat. Commun. 12 (2021).").

To better understand the microstructure of the binary and ternary blends, we perform grazing incidence wide-angle X-ray scattering (GIWAXS). FigureВ [2F, G](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2) show the 2D GIWAXS data of binary blend (Fig.В [2F](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2)) and ternary blend (Fig.В [2G](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2)) films, with their cake cut profiles presented in Fig.В [2H](/articles/s41467-024-50894-w#Fig2). Note that the peak at 0.47вҖүГ…вҲ’1 is from the Kapton foil, which is used to seal the measurement chamber. Both samples show a (100) scattering peak at 0.30вҖүГ…вҲ’1, which is ascribed to the lamellar stacking of PM6. ПҖ-ПҖ stacking peaks are observed at 1.70вҖүГ…вҲ’1 in the binary blend and at 1.73вҖүГ…вҲ’1 in the ternary blend. A slight enhancement and peak shift in the ПҖ-ПҖ stacking peak is observed in PM6:O-IDTBR:Y6 ternary system from 1.70вҖүГ…вҲ’1 to 1.73вҖүГ…вҲ’1 in the out-of-plane (OOP) direction, corresponding to a shrinkage of ПҖ-ПҖ distance from 3.70вҖүГ… to 3.63вҖүГ…. Both the increased intensity and peak shift to a higher q value are beneficial for the OPV use as a closer ПҖ-ПҖ stacking distance and more crystallites can cause faster and more efficient charge carrier transfer and transport.

--- SOURCE 2: [PDF] Flexible Solar-energy Harvesting System on Plastic with Thin-film ... ---
URL: http://www.princeton.edu/~nverma/VermaLabSite/Publications/2012/HuRieutortHuangSanzWagnerSturmVerma_CICC2012.pdf

SUMMARY:
Delivering power to an inductively-coupled load through 3cm-radius single- and double-layer inductors, the measured power-transfer efficiency is 22.6% and 31%.

FULL CONTENT:
Flexible Solar-energy Harvesting System on Plastic with Thin-film LC Oscillators Operating Above ft for Inductively-coupled Power Delivery Yingzhe Hu, Warren Rieutort-Louis, Liechao Huang, Josue Sanz-Robinson Sigurd Wagner, James C. Sturm, Naveen Verma Princeton University, Princeton (NJ), USA Abstract- This paper presents an energy-harvesting system consisting of amorphous-silicon (a-Si) solar cells and thin-film-transistor (TFT) power circuits on plastic. Along with patterned planar inductors, the TFTs realize an LC-oscillator that provides power inversion of the DC solar-module output, enabling a low-cost sheet for inductively-coupled wireless charging of devices. Despite the low performance of the TFTs (аўҢаўҡ=1.3MHz at a voltage of 15V), the oscillator can operate above 2MHz by incorporating the device parasitics into the resonant tank. This enables increased quality factor for the planar inductors, improving the power-transfer efficiency and the power delivered. Delivering power to an inductively-coupled load through 3cm-radius single- and double-layer inductors, the measured power-transfer efficiency is 22.6% and 31% (approaching the analytically-predicted bound), while the power delivered is 20mW and 22mW. I. INTRODUCTION Thin-film electronics can be processed at low temperatures enabling the integration of diverse transducers with functional circuits on large, flexible sheets. This can lead to transformational systems for high-resolution sensing [1] and energy harvesting [2]. A critical limitation, however, is the low performance of thin-film transistors (TFTs), which typically have ЭӮ аҜ§ around 1MHz. LC oscillators are a key building block in circuits for communication, control, and power conversion. An important characteristic is that they can operate above ЭӮ аҜ§ and could thus play a key role in overcoming the limitations of thin-film systems. In this work, we present a thin-film energy-harvesting system based on amorphous-silicon (a-Si) solar modules and TFTs. In contrast to previous work [2], we demonstrate energy-harvesting devices and power circuits in the same technology, thus enabling a path to low-cost, fully-integrated sheets for ubiquitous power conversion and delivery. Such sheets could be applied onto existing surfaces to convert them into charging stations for wireless powering of mobile devices. Compared to other designs [3], the proposed approach uses inductors rather than capacitors for coupling power to load devices. This enables greater power transfer for a given size (i.e., by a factor of 200 compared to [3]) as well as superior robustness to variations in proximity [4]. The key challenge with inductors, however, is reduced quality factor at low frequencies. We thus employ a thin-film LC oscillator as a power inverter to convert the DC solar-module outputs into AC waveforms, enabling inductive coupling at higher frequencies than that limited by ЭӮ аҜ§. The resulting topology also avoids the need for explicit switching control circuits, thus overcoming limitations to both performance and efficiency imposed by the TFTs. In the following sections, we analyze circuit tradeoffs, taking into account inherent component-level parasitics, which lead to a design-optimization methodology. II. SYSTEM OVERVIEW Fig. 1 shows a block diagram of the energy-harvesting system. The solar module consists of solar cells in series operating at an output voltage Ьё аҜўаҜЈ of 10-25V. Although LC oscillators offer the benefits mentioned, in order to function, they must have sufficient gain to meet the positive-feedback condition. This depends partially on device characteristics related to ЭӮ аҜ§ (i.e., transconductance and capacitances), which are not favorable for TFTs; however, it also depends on the inductance-to-resistance ratio achievable with patterned planar inductors. We thus exploit the ability to create physically-large inductors on plastic, which can lead to large values of this ratio, enabling oscillations despite poor TFT characteristics. Fig. 1: Block diagram of thin-film energy-harvesting system. Fig. 2 (left) shows the LC-oscillator including the circuit parasitics of importance. In addition to the TFT capacitances, the TFT gate resistances (ЬҙаҜҡаҜ”аҜ§аҜҳ) and inductor resistances (ЬҙаҜңаҜЎаҜ—) are included. The gate resistances and capacitances are modeled as lumped elements since the frequencies of interest are much lower than that due to the associated time constant (which is several giga-Hertz). Nonetheless, ЬҙаҜҡаҜ”аҜ§аҜҳ is significant for the analysis due to the use of a bottom-gate TFT structure (as described in Sec. IV), which requires thin gate metallization in order to ensure reliable gate dielectric formation. At resonance, the parasitics can be represented as shown in Fig. 2 (right), where ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ can be estimated by ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘаөҺЬіа¬¶аө«ЬҙаҜңаҜЎаҜ—аө…ЬҙаҜҡаҜ”аҜ§аҜҳаөҜаөҢ а° а°®аҜ…а°® аҜӢаі”аіҷаіҸа¬ҫаҜӢаі’аіҢаіҹаіҗаөҢ аҜ… а®јаіҒаіҢаіқбҲәаҜӢаі”аіҷаіҸа¬ҫаҜӢаі’аіҢаіҹаіҗбҲ», (1) and ЬҘаҜүаҜ”аҜҘcan be estimated by taking into account Miller multiplication effects: ЬҘаҜүаҜ”аҜҘаөҢ2 аөҲаө«ЬҘаҜҡаҜ—а¬өаө…ЬҘаҜҡаҜ—а¬¶аөҜаө…ЬҘаҜҡаҜҰа¬ө,а¬¶аө…ЬҘаҜўаҜ«аөҺ5 аөҲЬҘаҜўаҜ©аө…ЬҘаҜўаҜ«. (2) Fig. 2: LC oscillator with parasitics and equivalent circuit for analysis. With TFT output resistances being much larger than typical values for ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ, the positive-feedback condition requires that ЭғаҜ аөҲЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘаөҗ1, leading to the following requirement: аҜҡаіҳ а®јаіҒаіҢаіқаөҲ аҜ… бҲәаҜӢаі”аіҷаіҸа¬ҫаҜӢаі’аіҢаіҹаіҗбҲ»аөҗ1. (3) While the first term on the left represents a dependence related to device ЭӮ аҜ§, the second term suggests that this can be overcome by a suitable inductor. Fig. 3 plots Ь®/бҲәЬҙаҜңаҜЎаҜ—аө…ЬҙаҜҡаҜ”аҜ§аҜҳбҲ» values, both from measurement (using prototyped inductors) and from simulated prediction (using [5]). Fabricated TFTs achieve a measured ЭӮ аҜ§ of 1.3MHz (i.e., ЭғаҜ /ЬҘаҜЈаҜ”аҜҘаөҢ 4.2 аөҲ 10а¬ә rad/s ) at 15V, thus suggesting that the oscillation condition of Eq. 3 can be met with sufficient margin. Fig. 3: L/(Rind+Rgate) ratios for prototyped planar inductors. III. POWER INVERTER ANALYSIS AND OPTIMIZATION Given the viability of thin-film LC oscillators, this section analyzes their efficiency and power-transfer capabilities when used as power inverters with an inductively-coupled load. As shown in Fig. 2, power is delivered to a single load by coupling the inductors in the two LC tank branches. The load causes an associated resistance to be reflected to the oscillator outputs. For analysis, as in Fig. 4, we use an equivalent topology, with equal-valued inductors for each oscillator branch, independently coupled to load inductors with nearly-perfect coupling efficiency (i.e., kвүҲ1, which is reasonable for proximity power transfer). A reflected load (ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—) hence appears in each branch. This has two effects: (1) ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— impacts the positive-feedback condition, effectively altering the oscillation amplitude; and (2) ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— splits the power extracted from the LC tank with ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ in order to achieve the desired power delivery. These effects result in a design optimization methodology for maximum output power and efficiency. To analyze this, we define an oscillator strength parameter S; S is derived from the oscillation condition, and, as described in the following subsections, it suggests how the designer-selectable parameters, ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— and ЭғаҜ , should be set to maximize output power and efficiency: ЬөаөҢЭғаҜ аөҲбҲәЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—ЧҰ ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘбҲ». (4) An additional parameter of importance is the operating-point voltage of the solar-module ( Ьё аҜўаҜЈ), which affects both the absolute output power and efficiency that can be achieved. Fig. 4: Oscillator circuit with reflected load resistance; parameter values from a fabricated sample are shown for illustration. A. Power-transfer Optimization This section describes how the designer-selectable parameters should be set to optimally achieve a desired output power. For each oscillator branch, the power delivered to the load is: ЬІаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—аөҢ аҜҸаІҪаіҡаіҢаіҸа°® а¬¶аөҲаҜӢаІҪаіҡаіҢаіҸ, (5) where Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— is the amplitude of the AC oscillations. Eq. 5 suggests that ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— should be minimized for maximum power transfer; however, due to its impact on S, ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— affects Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—. In order to sustain oscillations, reducing ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— thus requires gm to be increased. This can be achieved in two ways: (1) by increasing Ьё аҜўаҜЈ, which raises the gate-overdrive of the TFTs; or (2) by increasing the W/L of the TFTs. Fig. 5(a) shows the effect of increasing Ьё аҜўаҜЈ (based on transistor-level simulations using extracted SPICE Level 61 models for the TFTs; measurement results are also shown for Ьё аҜўаҜЈаөҢ20Ьё to illustrate validity of the models). Due to the increased ЭғаҜ , nearly full-swing oscillations can be achieved at reduced values of ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— (as determined by S). Although increasing ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— increases Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— further, the effect saturates once sufficient S is achieved. Combined with the inverse dependence in Eq. 5, Fig. 5(b) shows that an optimal point thus occurs by balancing ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—. The resulting optimal ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— shown corresponds to a value of SвүҲ3.5; however, as discussed in Sec. III-B, efficiency optimization requires a higher value (SвүҲ5) for Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— saturation. Nonetheless, with regards to ЬІаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—, increasing Ьё аҜўаҜЈ also increases the achievable Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—, causing the effect in Fig. 6(a), where ЬІаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— scales roughly cubically (as predicted by Eq. 5). (a) VLoad vs. RLoad. (b) PLoad vs. RLoad. Fig. 5: Effect of RLoad scaling on oscillation amplitude and output power. Similarly, increasing W/L increases ЭғаҜ , enabling oscillations to be sustained at a lower ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—. However, raising W/L also increases ЬҘаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ, thereby reducing ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ in proportion. (a) PLoad vs. Vop (b) PLoad vs. W/L Fig. 6: Scaling of output power through designer-selectable parameters. As a result, S remains unchanged, causing Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— to also remain unchanged. As shown in Fig. 6(b), ЬІаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— thus scales linearly (due to ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—). B. Efficiency Optimization This section first examines the power consumed by the LC oscillator, and then uses this with the output power to analyze the power-transfer efficiency. Although the solar moduleвҖҷs output voltage ( Ьё аҜўаҜЈ) and current are coupled, the circuit operates in a region close to the open-circuit module voltage, hence we approximate the average power consumed as ЬІ аҜ”аҜ©аҜҡаөҢЬё аҜўаҜЈаөҲЬ«аҜҚа®ҝаҜҚ,аҜ”аҜ©аҜҡ, (6) where Ь«аҜҚа®ҝаҜҚ,аҜ”аҜ©аҜҡ is the average current drawn by each TFT. During each cycle, the Ьё аҜҡаҜҰ and Ьё аҜ—аҜҰ of the TFTs oscillate in counter phase. For large oscillations of Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—, the instantaneous TFT current (Ь«аҜҚа®ҝаҜҚ) is thus near zero both when Ьё аҜҡаҜҰ is maximum (due to low Ьё аҜ—аҜҰ) and when Ьё аҜ—аҜҰ is maximum (due to low Ьё аҜҡаҜҰ). (7) Fig. 7(b) shows Ь«аҜҚа®ҝаҜҚ,аҜ”аҜ©аҜҡ with respect to ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— (plotted for the circuit parameters in Fig. 4). Ь«аҜҚа®ҝаҜҚ,аҜ”аҜ©аҜҡ begins to saturate as ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— is increased. Now, the oscillator efficiency can be expressed as follows ЯҹаөҢ аіҮаІҪаіҡаіҢаіҸа°® а°®аөҲаіғаІҪаіҡаіҢаіҸ аҜҸ аіҡаіӣаҜӮаі…аІ·аі…,аіҢаіЎаі’аөҢ аө¬ аіҮаІҪаіҡаіҢаіҸа°® а°®аөҲаө«аіғаІҪаіҡаіҢаіҸЧҰаіғаіӣаіҢаіқаөҜаө° аҜҸ аіҡаіӣаҜӮаі…аІ·аі…,аіҢаіЎаі’ аөҲ аҜӢаіҒаіҢаіқ аҜӢаіҒаіҢаіқа¬ҫаҜӢаІҪаіҡаіҢаіҸаөҢЯҹаҜўаҜҰаҜ–аөҲЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ, (8) where two effective efficiencies are explicitly defined: ЯҹаҜўаҜҰаҜ– corresponds to the efficiency of maintaining oscillations in the presence of ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ and ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— (i.e., ensuring sufficient S); and ЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ corresponds to the efficiency with which current is delivered to ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— versus ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ. From Eq. 8, the impact on О· due to ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— and ЭғаҜ  can be understood. In addition to its explicit impact, ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— has an implicit effect through Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— and thus Ь«аҜҚа®ҝаҜҚ,аҜ”аҜ©аҜҡ; the profile in Fig. 5(a) shows the saturating effect of ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— on Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—. On the other hand, increasing ЭғаҜ  through W/L sizing causes a linear increase in ЬІаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— (Fig. 6(b)), but also a linear increase in Ь«аҜҚа®ҝаҜҚ,аҜ”аҜ©аҜҡ, due to TFT width scaling. As a result, О· exhibits no net change with W/L. To understand the effect of increasing Ьё аҜўаҜЈ, it is helpful to look at ЯҹаҜўаҜҰаҜ– and ЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ separately. However, the reduction allowed to ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—, improves ЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ. As a result, the overall О· initially improves, as shown in Fig. 7(b); however, this improvement eventually saturates according to the expression for ЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ. (a) О· vs. RLoad. (b) О· vs.Vop. Fig. 8: Simulations of harvester efficiency with respect to RLoad and Vop. Given these effects, we can estimate a bound for the maximum overall efficiency achievable by the topology. Using Eqs. 4, 7, and 8, and assuming, Ьё аҜўаҜЈ>>Ьё аҜ§ and Ьё аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—вүҲЬё аҜўаҜЈ ЯҹаҜ аҜ”аҜ«аөҺ аҜҸ аіҡаіӣа°®аөҲаі’аіҳ аі„ а¬¶аҜҸ аіҡаіӣаөҲ1 4ЭғЭүЬёЭӢЭҢ аөҢ а¬¶ аҜҢаөҺ40% , (9) where SвүҲ5 has been used based on discussed optimizations. IV. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS The energy-harvesting system is fabricated on 50Ојm-thick polyimide foil using patterned inductors of several sizes (i.e., radii of 2, 2.5, 3cm). All measurements are performed with an inductively-coupled load at a distance of 1mm. A sample photograph is in Fig. 9. TFTs with W/L of 3600Ојm/6Ојm are used with a layout optimized for minimum ЬҘаҜҡаҜҰ, ЬҘаҜҡаҜ—, since maximizing ЭғаҜ /ЬҘаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ is critical for the positive-feedback condition (Eq. 3). TFTs with 5Вөm gate-source/drain overlap, give a measured ЭӮ аҜ§ of 1.3MHz (at 15V). Further reduction in overlap is unreliable for lithographic alignment on plastic. Fig 9: Sample inductively-coupled, energy-harvesting system on plastic. Typical oscillator waveforms from measurement are in Fig. 10; while nearly full swing oscillations are achieved at 2MHz, oscillations with reduced amplitude are also observed at 3.64MHz. A performance summary is shown in Table I. Fig. 10: Measured waveforms from system captured via oscilloscope. Table I: Prototype system performance (shown for Vop =15V, 25V). Inductor size radius= 2, 2.5, 3cm TFT W/L 3600Ојm / 6Ојm ft 1.3MHz (Vop=15V) Max. Freq. 2.1MHz (VLoad=16V), 3.64MHz (VLoad=5V) Coupling distance to load 1mm Vop 15 25 25 (2-layer inductor) Solar Modules 180cm2 300cm2 300cm2 Optimal RLoad 20kО© 8kО© 2kО© Max. PLOAD 2.6mW 20.3mW 22.1mW Max. О· 15.4% 22.6% 31% Fig. 11 shows the structure of a-Si NMOS TFTs as well as a measured typical I-V characteristic. Fabrication is achieved by PECVD deposition at low temperature (180В°C), enabling the use of a polyimide foil substrate. For robustness under flexing, metal layers use chrome-aluminum-chrome stacks. Fig. 11: Low-temperature processing of a-Si NMOS TFTs. A. Power and Overall Efficiency versus RLoad The measured output power delivered to a load (ЬІаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—) and the overall efficiency (О·) are shown in Fig. 12. Optimal values exist, as described in Sec. III-A & III-B. Additionally, physically-larger inductors lead to larger ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ, improving ЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ, and thereby increasing the overall efficiency. (a) PLoad vs. RLoad. (b) О· vs. RLoad. Fig. 12: Output power and efficiency measurements with RLoad. B. Power and Overall Efficiency versus Vop Fig. 13(a) shows ЬІ аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— with respect to Ьё аҜўаҜЈ (for an optimal ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—), Fig. 13(b) shows О· with respect to Ьё аҜўаҜЈ and ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—, and Fig. 13(c) shows the optimal О· with respect to Ьё аҜўаҜЈ (for an inductor radius of 3cm). As described in Sec. III-A, Fig. 13(a) shows that ЬІ аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— increases approximately cubically with Ьё аҜўаҜЈ. Larger inductors result in improved ЬІ аҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ— for the same reason as in Section IV-A. Further, as mentioned in Sec. III-B, О· initially increases with Ьё аҜўаҜЈ due to resulting improvement in ЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ through reduced ЬҙаҜ…аҜўаҜ”аҜ—. Since Fig. 13(c) approaches the voltage limit of the TFTs, to further explore this effect, we increase ЯҹаҜ§аҜ”аҜЎаҜһ by explicitly increasing ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ. ЬҙаҜүаҜ”аҜҘ can be increased by further increasing the inductor size or by stacking inductors in multiple layers; this causes the number of turns to effectively double, increasing L by a factor of four, while causing ЬҙаҜңаҜЎаҜ— to increase by only a factor of two. The third column of Table I shows the measured results, demonstrating power-transfer efficiency approaching the bound of Eq. 9. (a) PLoad vs. Vop (b) О· vs. RLoad. (c) О· vs. Vop Fig. 13: Efficiency measurements with respect to Vop. V. CONCLUSIONS Thin-film systems face performance limitations due to the ЭӮ аҜ§ limit of the TFTs. LC oscillators can resonate device capacitances with the tank inductors, enabling operation beyond the ЭӮ аҜ§. This, however, requires inductors with sufficiently small resistances. Thin-film systems fabricated on plastic substrates can achieve this through physically-large planar inductors that can be patterned on large substrates. We thus demonstrate operation of a thin-film LC oscillator above device ЭӮ аҜ§. We exploit this to then create an energy-harvesting system that uses an LC-oscillator-based power inverter to convert a DC solar-module output into an AC waveform for inductively-coupled wireless power delivery to load devices. The oscillator operates at a maximum frequency of 2.1MHz, and the system achieves an output power of up to 22mW, with a maximum power-transfer efficiency of 31%, which approaches the predicted maximum value for the topology. enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements in flexible energy harvesting devices have achieved power conversion efficiencies exceeding 16%, significantly enhancing the viability of wearable technologies.
Flexible energy harvesting-storage systems (FEHSS) can seamlessly integrate into wearable devices, providing a lightweight and efficient power source for various applications, including biosensors.
User feedback on integrated flexible devices highlights improved comfort and functionality, emphasizing the importance of mechanical compliance and constant power supply for wearable technology.
